name: Vaganski vrh (1757m) — Velebit's highest, from Velika Paklenica
description: The roof of Velebit (1757m), climbed up the shaded Velika Paklenica gorge to Planinarski dom, then steeply via Lipa staza to the summit ridge. A committing full-day stamina hike — NOT a scramble, no via-ferrata or airy ridges — with 300km views on a clear day. Pin = Paklenica NP entrance/upper car park; summit at 44.36732, 15.50456.
communityNotes: Hard by length and ascent (~1500m gain), not by exposure — good news for the heights-averse member. The crux is Lipa staza: 'very steep, there's a longish section with loose rocks, slippery and unstable but doable.' WATER is the real planning issue above the dom: 'can be very difficult in August if it's hot because you need to carry all the water' — refill at Lugarnica forest hut and Planinarski dom (both in/below the gorge) and the Marasovac well near the top, then carry your own. June: the gorge stays cool and shaded (a big advantage vs Biokovo); possible isolated snow patch just below the summit on north aspects, not a blocker. Many split it over two days with a night at the dom. SummitPost route note: 'From shelter Struge, 10 min to the Marasovac well, then right onto Vaganski vrh.'
timeNeeded: 9–11h full day (or 2 days with a night at the dom)
bestTime: Start at the gate by 7am; gorge is cool/shaded so heat is manageable. Carry your own water above the dom.

name: Premužić Trail (Premužićeva staza) — North Velebit, Zavižan → Rossi / Alan
description: Croatia's most-loved engineered drystone trail, threading the wild karst towers of the Rožanski and Hajdučki kukovi at a near-flat grade — a masterpiece of trail-building that even non-mountaineers can walk. Pin = Dom Zavižan trailhead; Zavižan→Alan is 16km point-to-point, but day-hikers do Zavižan→Rossi's shelter (44.7659, 14.9873) and back. CAVEAT: far north of Velebit, off our main corridor.
communityNotes: Uniquely friendly to the exposure-averse member: 'as there are virtually no steep ascents, the route is suitable for visitors unaccustomed to mountaineering' — walled and engineered along cliff edges, never a scramble. Near-universal acclaim: 'we are experienced hikers but this hike was maybe the best ever!' (AllTrails, 4.9★). The real challenges are LENGTH and total sun exposure: 'a long, exposed limestone traverse that will fry your brain if you underestimate sun, distance, or water.' NO drinkable water on the trail — fill at Zavižan hut, Rossi's shelter (rainwater, treat), Alan hut. Coldest/windiest spot in Croatia — pack a windproof even in summer. LOGISTICS: Zavižan is ~2h+ from Zadar toward Plitvice/Senj on a narrow mountain road; the full point-to-point needs a paid shuttle (~€90–100), so for our one-way car a Zavižan→Rossi's-shelter out-and-back (2–3h each way) is the sane day option. Only worth the northern detour if Plitvice is also a 'yes'. | Off-route: Premuzic trail is in North Velebit NP, 69km north. We hike Paklenica (South Velebit) on Day 1.
timeNeeded: Day-hike Zavižan→Rossi & back 4–6h; full Zavižan→Alan 5.5–7h one-way
bestTime: June is ideal; start early for the light on the kukovi, carry all water + a windproof

name: Tulove grede scramble (Winnetou rocks) — southern Velebit
description: A short, surreal scramble up the breccia towers used as Karl May's 'Winnetou' film set, reached via the historic Majstorska cesta. Big reward for little distance (~3km, ~260m gain, 1.5–2.5h) — but the final summit block is a genuine hands-on scramble with a fixed cable and narrow gaps. Pin = roadside parking layby on Majstorska cesta (rocks/summit at 44.26351, 15.65560, where a separate viewpoint pin sits).
communityNotes: Read this one carefully for the exposure-averse member: the walk-in is easy and otherworldly, but 'a hiking cable was installed... the path goes through narrow gaps where you can't pass with a backpack... at the end you'll have to climb and scramble a bit to reach the top — you'll use both arms and legs.' The approach views are already huge, so the nervous member can comfortably stop below the summit block while others top out. The access road is rough: 'narrow, unpaved and already an adventure in itself' — last ~6km gravel, fine for a careful driver but slow. June good; carry your own water (none on route). LANDMINE NOTE: the on-trail corridor is cleared but areas around Mali Alan remain mine-suspect — do NOT leave the marked path. Watch for vipers in summer.
bestTime: May–Oct; stay strictly on marked paths (mines off-trail), watch for vipers

name: Biokovo — Vošac (1421m) on foot from Makarska (± Sveti Jure)
description: The 'real' Biokovo hike on foot: a relentless ~1150m climb from Makar village above Makarska to the Vošac ridge (1421m) and its mountain hut, with the iconic Makarska-Riviera-from-above view. Half-day to the hut (5–6h RT); the long, hot plateau push onward to Sveti Jure (1762m) makes it a 9–11h day. Pin = Ivan Krstitelj church car park, Makar.
communityNotes: VERDICT on the brief's 'Sveti Jure on foot vs road': do Vošac on foot, drive the rest. Vošac is the prize at half the effort — 4.7★ across 140+ reviews. 'Exceptionally steep, a relentless climb'; 'the exposure is immediate and constant, with parts of the trail skirting steep drop-offs' — no scrambling/cables, but airy switchback sections worth flagging for the heights-averse member; loose rock makes the descent the slippery part. Above Vošac it's a long undulating plateau to Sveti Jure: 'apart from the fact it is all uphill it is a very clear path, no scrambling,' but 'hikers who walk all the way up reach the plateau and then see people who drove up and had a 5-minute walk.' HEAT is the killer: south-facing rock, almost no shade — 'start before 9am (ideally 06:30)'; allow 3L water each (the Toni Roso hut at Vošac has water/refreshments when open; nothing else on route). The toll-road pin reaches Sveti Jure + Skywalk for a fraction of the effort — so on a hot June day, hike Vošac and DRIVE Sveti Jure.
cost: Biokovo Nature Park pedestrian €15/adult Jun–Sep (€10 with mountaineering card); free parking at Makar. Some report Vošac sits at/below the fee gate — verify on arrival.
timeNeeded: Vošac 5–6h RT; continue to Sveti Jure = 9–11h full day
bestTime: Sunrise start essential in June (south wall, no shade); summit windy/cold — bring a layer

name: Sveti Ilija (961m) — highest peak of Pelješac, above Orebić
description: The shapely highest peak of the Pelješac peninsula (961m), a non-technical but sustained climb straight up from sea level with a 360° payoff over Korčula, the Adriatic islands and the mainland mountains. A natural fit for the post-pickup Pelješac/Korčula block. Pin = Orebić (trail starts above town); summit at 42.99629, 17.15953.
communityNotes: Several trailheads: the classic Orebić ascent is steep (~1080m gain, ~16km loop, 6h40, the best-rated at 4.8★/68) while the Gornja Nakovana pass start (near Nakovanj, 43.00369, 17.08355) is the shortest/easiest (~14km, ~700m gain, ~5h). Not technical but a real climb — 'long and a lot of elevation gain'; minimal shade on the upper karst so it's a hot-but-doable June morning hike, fine for the whole group (no scrambling). Carry 2–3L water (no reliable spring). Best done as a dawn or late-afternoon outing while basing in Orebić for the Korčula ferry. Skip if the islands block is cut or it's a heatwave — the south-facing rock bakes.
timeNeeded: 5–7h RT depending on trailhead (Nakovanj shorter, Orebić steeper)
bestTime: Dawn or late afternoon in June (little shade, hot rock); clear day for the island panorama

name: Zelengora Lakes (Orlovačko / Štirinsko / Kotlaničko) — Sutjeska NP
description: The gentle, soulful side of Sutjeska NP: a 'green mountain' plateau of glacial lakes with easy meadow-and-forest walks — the low-stress alternative to Maglić. Orlovačko jezero (1438m, pin here) is the most accessible; a relaxed circular route links several lakes with no exposure. CATCH: the access roads are 4x4-only.
communityNotes: 'Lakes, viewpoints, meadows and silence.' Orlovačko 'is the most accessible and most photographed — you only need to descend on foot, not far nor strenuous.' A multi-lake loop (Orlovačko–Štirinsko–Kotlaničko) is 'moderate, physical fitness required but previous experience is not necessary — a smooth circular route at a relaxed mountain pace'; Bregoč summit (2015m) ~1h40 'not technically demanding'; the Borić viewpoint from Donje Bare (~30 min) is 'the finest viewpoint on all of Zelengora.' ROAD REALITY: the R434 approaches from Foča/Kalinovik are 'totally unpaved, requiring a 4x4 — a bumpy, dusty ride of about an hour'; Donje Bare is 'accessible by off-road vehicles only.' A normal rental likely can't reach the trailheads in the wet — plan a 4x4 transfer from Foča/Tjentište or pick a dry day. June = lush green, wildflowers; swimming + camping permitted at the lakes.
cost: Park entry 10 KM (~€5) pp; 4x4 transfer extra if no off-road car
timeNeeded: Half day (single lake) to full day (multi-lake loop)
bestTime: June for green meadows + flowers; pick a dry day for the dirt road
facilities: Lakeside wild camping; Orlovačko has a mountain hut; swimming OK

name: Prenj — Zelena Glava (2155m), 'Himalayas of Herzegovina'
description: The highest, wildest peak near Konjic — dramatic and alpine. HONEST VERDICT FOR THIS GROUP: not a casual day hike. Doable in a long day from the high Tisovica valley (~1450m, pin here) but with a via-ferrata/cable scramble to the summit, fog-and-navigation risk on a karst labyrinth, and Prenj's notorious lack of water. A guide is strongly recommended; the exposure-averse member would dislike the summit pitch.
communityNotes: ACCESSIBILITY: 'hiking the high peaks of Prenj is generally not recommended for absolute beginners without a guide.' From Tisovica ~2–3h moderate walking, then 'once under the Zelena Glava cliff, the next hour will be a steep climb, at places using ferrata until you are at the top.' Fit non-alpinists CAN do it as a long day, but the summit is the technical crux. WATER (verified — carry a LOT): 'Prenj is dry and thirsty, swallowing rain into subterranean channels — carry at least 3–4 litres per person per day, springs are rare and often dry up in summer.' NAVIGATION: 'fog can descend rapidly, turning the limestone labyrinth into a dangerous trap' — GPS + guide advised. JUNE: 'before June snow can still block the higher passes'; mid-late June usually clearing — verify. ROAD: the Tisovica trailhead needs a 'robust off-road vehicle — rough macadam, unsuitable for smaller vehicles'; a normal rental CAN'T get up, use a Konjic operator 4x4 transfer (gentler asphalt start at Rujište, 1050m, is a much longer ascent). UXO note: stick strictly to marked trails.
cost: Free trail; 4x4 transfer to Tisovica via a Konjic operator (paid)
timeNeeded: Long full day from Tisovica; or overnight at Jezerce hut
bestTime: Late June onward (snow clears); dawn start, carry 3–4L water pp, take a guide

name: Bjelašnica Summit (2067m) from Babin Do
description: A short, easy 2000er near the Olympic ski resort — distinct from the existing Umoljani→Lukomir rim hike. From the Babin Do ski base you climb open ski-slope/ridge terrain to the 2067m summit and its old Olympic observatory. Quick, no exposure, big 360° views — a low-commitment add-on if based around Bjelašnica/Lukomir, with the easiest trailhead access of any hike here (paved road).
communityNotes: The short, sharp contrast to the long Umoljani–Lukomir canyon-rim hike already in the data. From Babin Do the ascent is ~1.5–2h up grassy/rocky slopes (the ski infrastructure means it's never remote or technical), summit marked by the abandoned Olympic weather observatory. No scrambling or exposure — fine for the whole group including the heights-averse member, and easy to bail on. Best as a half-day or sunrise jaunt if already overnighting near Bjelašnica/Lukomir; otherwise the Umoljani–Lukomir rim hike is the more rewarding outing. Road to Babin Do is paved (developed resort) so a normal car is fine.
bestTime: Clear day for the 360° panorama; sunrise option

name: Čvrsnica — Hajdučka vrata rock arch (Blidinje Nature Park)
description: Bosnia's most striking natural monument: a 5m-diameter stone arch perched at ~2000m on Mt Čvrsnica, framing the Herzegovina mountains. A scenic medium-difficulty hike (~3.5–4h one-way) from the Blidinje plateau, often combined with the Veliki Vilinac peak (2118m, 43.62735, 17.61951). Pin = the arch; trailhead is the Blidinje/Risovac–Muharnica area (~43.66, 17.55).
communityNotes: 'One of the most beautiful hiking trails on Čvrsnica passes from Vitlenica to Hajdučka vrata, about 4 hours one-way.' Medium difficulty; from the Vilinac/Hajdučka-vrata junction (1860m) it's under an hour to the arch. Protected geomorphological monument since 1985 and 'very fragile' — photograph from several metres away, don't stand under it. The Vilinac mountain lodge and a shelter at Zaglavlje sit on the trail for weather cover. Great pairing with the Mostar→Konjic corridor and a Blidinje overnight; the plateau (Croatia's/Bosnia's high karst) is cool even in summer. Trailhead access via the Risovac/Dugo Polje park entrance (paved to the plateau, then forest road) — confirm the exact Muharnica start and road condition locally before committing a normal car.
cost: Blidinje Nature Park entry (small fee); free trail
bestTime: June–Sept; cool high plateau, clear day for the arch + Vilinac views

name: Veliki Međed (2287m) — the planned Black Lake → Međed ridge hike (Durmitor)
description: THE GROUP'S PLANNED DURMITOR ROUTE — Black Lake up to the twin Mali + Veliki Međed summits on the most photographed peak in the park. Verdict: do it, but it's an honest scramble, not a walk: ~11–13.7km, ~900–960m gain, 6–7.5h, with UIAA-I scrambling, fixed cables and an exposed grassy spine. Pin = Black Lake / Ivan Do NP portal & parking.
communityNotes: HONEST EXPOSURE READ: 'expect several extremely steep sections where you'll scramble up with hands and feet, a short but near-vertical rock climb, several exposed rock ledges with sharp drop-offs and a few scree crossings... if you get nervous around heights or would struggle to hoist yourself up a rock face, this may not be the trail for you' (The Sandy Feet). Outdooractive: 'steep exposed grassy slopes and light but exposed climbing at UIAA grade I... when wet and/or with strong wind, there is absolute risk of falling.' Low fixed cables secure the worst of the spine. RECOMMENDATION: the three exposure-OK hikers do Međed; the heights-averse member does Planinica or the Škrka lakes from the same Black Lake start. JUNE SNOW: Međed (2287m) is lower and more grass/east-facing than Bobotov Kuk, melts out earlier — no reports of blocking snowfields here; the decisive risk is WET/WIND on the exposed spine, not snow. Go only on a dry, low-wind morning; skip in rain. WATER: fountain at the Black Lake trailhead (fill up); none reliable on route — carry 2L pp. Don't combine with Bobotov Kuk — pick ONE big exposed day. IMPORTANT: the existing 'me-durmitor-black-lake' pin is the LAKE; the Međed summit is at 43.12796, 19.06832, ~3km south up the ridge.
cost: Durmitor NP €5 pp/day (warden) + €2 parking at Black Lake
bestTime: Dry, low-wind morning only — the exposed grassy spine is dangerous wet/windy

name: Savin Kuk (2313m) — easiest high Durmitor summit (on foot or chairlift)
description: The most accessible of Durmitor's big peaks (2313m), reached on foot from Black Lake via the old 'King Nikola' route (~11–12km, ~900–1000m, 5.5–6.5h) or by the Savin Kuk ski chairlift from Virak (pin here) + a short walk. The standard out-and-back is a wide, non-exposed path — fine for the heights-averse member if you skip the Sljeme loop.
communityNotes: 'One of the most popular peaks on Durmitor and one of the easiest to get to — a wide and a little bit rocky/gravel path to the top' (SummitPost). The ONLY exposed bit is the cabled 'via ferrata' traverse toward Sljeme: 'a steel cable about 100m down, short but very steep and exposed' — that's the loop variant; the normal up-and-back avoids it entirely. It's the windiest peak in Durmitor: 'gusts strong enough to require stopping and crouching low' above the treeline — skip in strong wind. WATER: excellent — Savina Voda, the highest spring on Durmitor (2212m), drinkable, right below the summit. CHAIRLIFT CAVEAT: a fatal chairlift malfunction occurred here on 20 Dec 2025; the ~35-year-old lifts' maintenance 'requires improvisation' — treat summer-2026 lift operation as uncertain, plan to hike on foot and only count on the lift if you confirm it's running locally. June: normal route 'generally snow-free in summer.'
cost: Durmitor NP €5 pp/day; chairlift ~€8–12 pp return if running
timeNeeded: On foot from Black Lake 5.5–6.5h RT; with the lift ~1–2h on top
bestTime: Calm day (windiest peak); mid-June–mid-Sept; normal route avoids the cabled Sljeme traverse

name: Planinica (2330m) — the big-view, NON-technical Durmitor day
description: The answer for the exposure-averse member: a 2330m alpine plateau with 'arguably the best view in the entire park' — straight down onto the Škrka amphitheatre, Prutaš opposite and Bobotov Kuk beyond — reached on a long but completely non-technical walk from Black Lake. ~18.5km RT, ~950m gain, 7.5–8h. Pin = summit; the hike starts at Black Lake (43.15233, 19.09718).
communityNotes: The standout less-hardcore peak day: 'while it involves a long 4-hour grind to reach the summit, there are no specifically challenging or technical sections along the way' (The Sandy Feet). The difficulty is LENGTH, not terrain — the only hazard is one final switchback with 'a spray of ochre rubble that feels somewhat unstable.' Reward: 'a high alpine plateau that boasts arguably the best view in the entire park,' with wild horses, goats and near-total solitude (far fewer people than Bobotov Kuk): 'a feeling of complete isolation, ideal for those who want a full half-day hike with rewarding views but without the crowds.' This is the everyone-can-do-it Durmitor summit while the scramblers tackle Veliki Međed from the same Black Lake start. JUNE: south-ish plateau melts relatively early, no dated snow issues found. WATER: not reliably on route — carry 2L pp. Long exposure to weather on the open plateau — skip in a washout (no cover).
cost: Durmitor NP €5 pp/day + €2 parking at Black Lake
bestTime: Clear day for the Škrka/Bobotov panorama; not in a washout (open, exposed plateau)

name: Škrčka jezera (Škrka lakes) — glacial lakes you can camp at (Durmitor)
description: Two glacial lakes (~1700m) cradled in 'probably the most beautiful part of the Durmitor massif,' with a mountain lodge and a tent meadow between them — a legit lakeside overnight. ~10.5km RT / ~836m / 6–7h to the lakes, non-exposed. Pin = Veliko Škrčko jezero; trailheads off the P14 Sedlo road (Dobri Do, the easier southern approach, or Todorov Do).
communityNotes: 'An ideal location for tent camping, swimming during the summer months, watching wild mountain goats and deer' (TO Žabljak). CAMPING: 'Planinarski dom Škrka, a rudimentary hiker's cabin... camping is permitted in the surrounding meadows, a freshwater spring is available behind the hut,' open mid-June to end-September — so you can camp lakeside, do an easy day in, and peak the next morning. The lakes-only route is moderate and non-exposed; pairing with Prutaš (2393m) adds an airy, secured ridge (see notes) — for a relaxed lakes day, go direct and skip the Prutaš summit. JUNE: lakes at ~1700m melt early and are swimmable in summer; a June visitor hit only 'snowdrift patches near the summit, not impassable' — the lakes are fine, only the high passes hold snow. Best-watered hike in Durmitor (springs at the hut and through the valley). Better as an overnight or dedicated long day than squeezed into a single Durmitor day.
cost: Durmitor NP €5 pp/day; free to camp at the lodge meadow
timeNeeded: 6–7h RT to the lakes; ideal as a 1-night camp
bestTime: Mid-June onward (lodge opens, lakes melted); dry conditions for the Prutaš extension
facilities: Planinarski dom Škrka (basic cabin) + tent meadow + freshwater spring; swimming in summer

name: Štirovnik (1749m) — Lovćen's true summit hike from Ivanova Korita
description: The actual highest peak of Mt Lovćen (1749m, 92m higher than the Njegoš Mausoleum), reached on a waymarked trail from the Ivanova Korita picnic area (~1390m). Pine forest and scrub give way to open karst and a trig point with views arguably broader than the mausoleum's — and almost no crowds. The easy-moderate Lovćen hiking option. Pin = summit; trailhead Ivanova Korita ~42.3803, 18.8320.
communityNotes: A gentle-to-moderate half-day with a big payoff and few people: ~6–10km return, ~360–400m gain, 3–4h, 'moderate,' through pine forest then open karst to a cairn/trig point. 'It receives significantly fewer visitors than the mausoleum despite being 92 metres higher,' with views 'arguably broader than from the mausoleum, without the crowds.' Note a comms/military tower on the very top — the panorama is from around/just below it. No scrambling — suitable for the exposure-averse member, and pairs naturally with driving up to the mausoleum the same day. June: snow-free at this altitude, but exposed karst with little shade above the treeline — go in the morning and carry water (no reliable spring on the ridge).
cost: Lovćen NP entry ~€3–5/person (gate); no extra hiking fee
bestTime: Clear morning — exposed karst, little shade; haze builds in afternoon heat

name: Kom Vasojevićki (2461m) — Komovi summit from Eko Katun Štavna
description: The most popular Komovi summit (2461m), climbed from the Eko Katun Štavna plateau (~1700m) in far-eastern Montenegro — the group's backup wish. A wildflower-meadow start steepens up scree to a rocky summit ridge with one short exposed scrambling move. Pin = the Štavna katun/parking TRAILHEAD (OSM-verified); summit ~42.6922, 19.6683, ~2km south.
communityNotes: ~8.5km RT, ~765m gain, 4.5–5h (plan ~5h with breaks). Officially 'moderate' but with a sting: a short exposed section near the top where 'most will need their hands,' alongside 'a ravine that falls away sharply' — the EXPOSURE-AVERSE member should know about this one brief-but-real move, plus steep grassy slopes that 'do not tolerate any misstep when wet.' (The harder hands-on scramble described in some reports is for the neighbouring Kučki Kom — Vasojevićki is the easier of the two.) WATER: 'four icy springs' at the Štavna trailhead — refill before climbing, none high up. ROAD (the crux for a rental): from Trešnjevik pass, turn onto a rough ~4.5km dirt track ('large rocks, uneven surfaces, eroded dips') — 'manageable in a small 2WD with a lot of patience and deliberate manoeuvring'; ~2h from Kolašin/the coast, so it eats most of a day. JUNE: wildflower meadows and 'peaceful trails,' but 'parts of the trail can still be covered with snow or mud near the higher peaks' — mid-to-late June safest. KATUN STAY: chalets reported (~€40/house of 5, hot showers) BUT one 2024 source says the original Eko Katun Štavna is closed — verify, or use Kobil Do / Eko Katun Martinovica 1750; plateau camping feasible. OVERLAP: the generic existing 'me-komovi' pin (42.6622,19.6433) is ~6km off the real trailhead — this entry has the correct access point.
cost: Free hike; Komovi park gate ~€2/person reported; Štavna katun huts ~€40/house (5) or ~€8 pp reported (confirm availability)
timeNeeded: ~5h hike + ~2h drive each way from Kolašin/coast
bestTime: Morning start; mid-to-late June safest for snow-free upper slopes
facilities: Štavna katun (chalets w/ hot-water showers reported, status uncertain) + free parking + ~4 spring water points at the trailhead

name: Rumija (1593m) — sea + Skadar double-view summit above Bar
description: Montenegro's southernmost big summit (1593m), on the ridge that separates the Adriatic from Skadar Lake — on a clear day you see both at once, plus the St Vladimir cross/chapel on top. Climbed from Stari Bar (~170m): a serious, long, hot deep-south day, not a casual outing. Pin = summit; trailhead at Stari Bar ~42.094, 19.134.
communityNotes: Honest reality check — this is BIG: the full Stari Bar → summit round trip is ~13.9km with ~1540m gain over ~8h (wibkestravels GPX); the Komoot loop variant is 22.7km / 9h+ and rated 'expert.' Not technically hard ('isn't particularly technically demanding, but it's long and has a lot of elevation gain') but a full, hot, low-shade slog through Mediterranean scrub into alpine terrain. The trail 'is less well-marked than national-park trails' — carry a GPX. WATER: bring 3L; a cold spring/fountain exists partway but don't rely on it in June heat. JUNE = the catch: starting at sea level near Bar means brutal lower-slope heat — dawn start or skip in a heatwave. Worth it only as a deep-south add-on if already basing near Bar/Ulcinj.
bestTime: Dawn start — sea-level heat makes a late start dangerous in June; clear day for the double sea+lake view
facilities: No facilities on the mountain; one cold spring/fountain partway (unreliable in summer); park at Stari Bar
